exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça o áudio e escolha o que você ouviu:
exercises.transcript: Apple
exercises.question: What word did you hear?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Apple' = maçã. Preste atenção ao som 'æ' no início — é um 'a' aberto, entre 'a' e 'é'.

exercises.transcript: Hospital
exercises.question: What word did you hear?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Hospital' — em inglês o acento é na primeira sílaba: HOS-pi-tal (não hos-pi-TAL como em português).

exercises.transcript: Beautiful
exercises.question: What word did you hear?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Beautiful' = bonito(a). Pronúncia: BIÚU-ti-fol (3 sílabas).

exercises.transcript: Dangerous
exercises.question: What word did you hear?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Dangerous' = perigoso. Pronúncia: DÊIN-djer-os.

exercises.transcript: University
exercises.question: What word did you hear?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'University' = universidade. Pronúncia: iu-ni-VÉR-si-ti.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a frase e escolha o significado:
exercises.transcript: Good morning. How are you today?
exercises.question: What did the person say?
options: A) Good night. How are you?
B) Good morning. How are you today?
C) Good afternoon. Where are you?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Good morning' = Bom dia. 'How are you today?' = Como você está hoje?

exercises.transcript: Excuse me, where is the nearest bus stop?
exercises.question: What is the person looking for?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Bus stop' = ponto de ônibus. 'Nearest' = mais próximo.

exercises.transcript: I would like to order a pizza, please.
exercises.question: What does the person want to do?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Order a pizza' = pedir uma pizza. 'I would like to' = Eu gostaria de.

exercises.transcript: The weather is very nice today.
exercises.question: What is the person talking about?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Weather' = tempo/clima. 'Very nice' = muito bom/agradável.

exercises.transcript: Can you help me find the library?
exercises.question: What does the person need help with?
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Library' = biblioteca. A pessoa precisa de ajuda para encontrar a biblioteca.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça e responda verdadeiro ou falso:
exercises.transcript: I went to the supermarket yesterday and bought some fruits and vegetables.
exercises.question: The person went to the supermarket today.
exercises.explanation_pt: Falso — a pessoa foi ONTEM (yesterday), não hoje.

exercises.transcript: The class starts at eight o'clock in the morning, not at nine.
exercises.question: The class starts at 9 AM.
exercises.explanation_pt: Falso — a aula começa às 8h, NÃO às 9h. O áudio diz 'not at nine'.

exercises.transcript: My sister is a dentist. She works at a clinic near our house.
exercises.question: The speaker's sister is a dentist.
exercises.explanation_pt: Verdadeiro — o áudio diz 'My sister is a dentist' = Minha irmã é dentista.

exercises.transcript: We are going to the beach next weekend if the weather is good.
exercises.question: They are going to the beach this weekend for sure.
exercises.explanation_pt: Falso — eles vão SE o tempo estiver bom ('if the weather is good'). Não é certeza.

exercises.transcript: The restaurant on Fifth Street closes at ten PM on weekdays.
exercises.question: The restaurant closes at 10 PM during the week.
exercises.explanation_pt: Verdadeiro — o restaurante fecha às 22h nos dias de semana (weekdays).

exercises.transcript: I have been studying for my exam all week. I feel confident about most of the topics, but I still need to review the last chapter. The exam is tomorrow at nine in the morning.
exercises.question: When is the exam?
exercises.explanation_pt: O áudio diz 'The exam is tomorrow at nine in the morning' = A prova é amanhã às 9h.

exercises.transcript: Our flight to New York leaves at six thirty in the evening. We need to arrive at the airport two hours before departure. That means we should leave home by three thirty at the latest.
exercises.question: What time should they leave home?
exercises.explanation_pt: Voo às 18h30, chegar 2h antes (16h30), sair de casa às 15h30 (three thirty).

exercises.transcript: The new coffee shop on Park Avenue is really popular. They serve organic coffee and homemade pastries. It's always crowded in the morning, so I usually go after lunch.
exercises.question: When does the speaker usually go to the coffee shop?
exercises.explanation_pt: O falante diz 'I usually go after lunch' = geralmente vou depois do almoço, porque de manhã está lotado.

exercises.transcript: My neighbor has a beautiful garden. She grows tomatoes, peppers, and herbs. Every Saturday, she gives us some fresh vegetables from her garden.
exercises.question: What does the neighbor give them?
exercises.explanation_pt: A vizinha dá vegetais frescos (fresh vegetables) do jardim dela todo sábado.

exercises.transcript: The city library has a new program for children. Every Wednesday afternoon, a volunteer reads stories to kids aged three to eight. It's free and no registration is needed.
exercises.question: How much does the program cost?
exercises.explanation_pt: O programa é gratuito (free) e não precisa de inscrição (no registration needed).

exercises.transcript: A: Excuse me, I'm looking for the science building. Can you help me? B: Sure! Go straight ahead and turn right at the second intersection. It's the large gray building on your left. A: Thank you so much. Is it far from here? B: Not at all. It's about a five-minute walk.
exercises.question: How far is the science building?
exercises.explanation_pt: B diz 'about a five-minute walk' = cerca de 5 minutos a pé.

exercises.transcript: A: Hi, I'd like to book a room for two nights, please. B: Of course. We have single and double rooms available. Would you prefer a room with a city view or a garden view? A: A garden view, please. How much is it per night? B: It's one hundred and twenty dollars per night, including breakfast.
exercises.question: How much is the room per night?
exercises.explanation_pt: O funcionário diz '$120 per night, including breakfast' = $120 por noite, incluindo café da manhã.

exercises.transcript: A: Good morning. I have an appointment with Dr. Santos at ten o'clock. B: Let me check... Yes, Dr. Santos will see you shortly. Please have a seat in the waiting area. A: Thank you. Should I fill out any forms? B: Yes, please complete this patient form and bring it back to the front desk.
exercises.question: What does the receptionist ask the patient to do?
exercises.explanation_pt: A recepcionista pede para preencher um formulário de paciente (patient form).

exercises.transcript: A: I can't believe how much traffic there is today. B: I know. There's construction on the highway, so everyone is taking side streets. A: Maybe we should take the subway next time. B: That's a good idea. It would be much faster and we wouldn't have to worry about parking.
exercises.question: Why is there so much traffic?
options: B) There's construction on the highway
exercises.explanation_pt: B explica que há construção/obras na highway (rodovia), então todos estão pegando ruas alternativas.

exercises.transcript: A: Professor, I have a question about the assignment. When you say we need three sources, do they all need to be academic journals? B: Not necessarily. At least two should be from peer-reviewed journals, but the third can be a reputable news source or a book. A: Got it. And what's the word limit? B: Between one thousand and fifteen hundred words, not including the bibliography.
exercises.question: How many sources need to be from academic journals?
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor diz 'at least two should be from peer-reviewed journals' = pelo menos duas devem ser de revistas acadêmicas.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça o diálogo no restaurante e responda:
exercises.transcript: A: Good evening. A table for two, please. B: Right this way, sir. Here's the menu. Can I start you off with some drinks? A: I'll have a glass of water, please. And could you tell me what the special is today? B: Today's special is grilled salmon with roasted vegetables.
exercises.question: What is today's special?
options: B) Grilled salmon with roasted vegetables
exercises.explanation_pt: O garçom diz 'grilled salmon with roasted vegetables' = salmão grelhado com vegetais assados.

exercises.transcript: A: Excuse me, where is the check-in counter for flights to Miami? B: International departures are on the second floor. Take the escalator on your right and follow the signs to Terminal B. A: Thank you. Do you know if there's a currency exchange nearby? B: Yes, there's one right next to the security checkpoint on the second floor.
exercises.question: Where are international departures?
exercises.explanation_pt: O funcionário diz 'International departures are on the second floor' = embarque internacional no segundo andar.

exercises.transcript: A: Hi, I'm checking in. The reservation is under the name Silva. B: Let me look that up... Yes, I have you down for three nights in a double room. Could I see your ID, please? A: Here you go. Is the Wi-Fi free? B: Yes, it's complimentary. Breakfast is served from six thirty to ten in the restaurant on the ground floor.
exercises.question: What time does breakfast end?
exercises.explanation_pt: O recepcionista diz 'Breakfast is served from six thirty to ten' = café da manhã das 6h30 às 10h.

exercises.transcript: A: Hello, I have a prescription from my doctor. Can you fill it? B: Of course, let me take a look. This will be ready in about fifteen minutes. Would you like to wait? A: Sure. While I'm here, do you have anything for a sore throat? B: Yes, I'd recommend these throat lozenges. Take one every four hours.
exercises.question: How long will the prescription take?
exercises.explanation_pt: O farmacêutico diz 'ready in about fifteen minutes' = pronto em cerca de 15 minutos.

exercises.transcript: A: Doctor, I've been having headaches almost every day this week. B: I see. Are they more in the front of your head or the back? A: Mostly in the front, behind my eyes. B: And how many hours of sleep are you getting? A: About five or six. I've been studying a lot for my exams. B: That's likely part of the problem. Sleep deprivation is one of the most common causes of tension headaches.
exercises.question: What does the doctor think is causing the headaches?
exercises.explanation_pt: O médico diz que privação de sono (sleep deprivation) é uma das causas mais comuns de dores de cabeça tensionais.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a conversa telefônica e responda:
exercises.transcript: A: Hello, this is the admissions office at Westfield University. How can I help you? B: Hi, I'm calling to ask about the application deadline for international students. A: The deadline for the fall semester is March fifteenth. You'll need to submit your application, transcripts, and TOEFL scores by that date. B: And what's the minimum TOEFL score required? A: We require a minimum of eighty on the TOEFL iBT, with no section below nineteen.
exercises.question: What is the minimum TOEFL score required?
exercises.explanation_pt: A atendente diz 'a minimum of eighty on the TOEFL iBT' = mínimo de 80 no TOEFL iBT.

exercises.transcript: A: Good afternoon, City Medical Center. How may I direct your call? B: Hi, I'd like to schedule an appointment with Dr. Patel, please. A: Sure. Dr. Patel has availability next Tuesday at two PM or Thursday at nine thirty AM. Which works better for you? B: Thursday morning would be great. A: All right, you're booked for Thursday at nine thirty.
exercises.question: When is the appointment?
exercises.explanation_pt: O paciente escolhe quinta-feira de manhã: Thursday at nine thirty AM.

exercises.transcript: A: Hello? B: Hey, it's me. I'm running a bit late. The bus broke down and I'm waiting for the next one. A: Oh no! How long do you think you'll be? B: Probably another twenty minutes. Can you let Professor Williams know I'll be a little late for the presentation?
exercises.question: Why is the person late?
exercises.explanation_pt: A pessoa diz 'The bus broke down' = o ônibus quebrou.

exercises.transcript: A: Thank you for calling TechSupport. My name is David. How can I assist you? B: Hi David, I'm having trouble connecting to the university Wi-Fi. It keeps saying authentication failed. A: I understand. Can you tell me your student ID number? B: It's two-zero-four-five-three-eight. A: Okay, I can see your account. It looks like your network password expired. I'll reset it now.
exercises.question: What was the problem?
options: C) The student ID is wrong
exercises.explanation_pt: O técnico diz 'your network password expired' = sua senha de rede expirou.

exercises.transcript: A: Hello, is this the housing office? B: Yes, it is. How can I help you? A: I'm a new international student and I'd like information about on-campus dormitories. B: Of course. We have single rooms for seven hundred dollars a month and shared rooms for four hundred and fifty. Both include Wi-Fi and access to the laundry facilities.
exercises.question: How much is a shared room per month?
exercises.explanation_pt: O funcionário diz 'shared rooms for four hundred and fifty' = quartos compartilhados por $450.

exercises.transcript: To get to the National Museum from here, walk straight down this road for about two blocks. When you reach the traffic light, turn left onto Oak Street. Walk past the post office and the bank. The museum will be on your right, just after the parking garage.
exercises.question: Where should you turn left?
exercises.explanation_pt: As direções dizem 'When you reach the traffic light, turn left' = quando chegar no semáforo, vire à esquerda.

exercises.transcript: To make a classic Brazilian lemonade, you'll need four limes, half a cup of sugar, three cups of cold water, and some ice. First, cut each lime into eight pieces. Then, put the limes, sugar, and water into a blender. Blend for about fifteen seconds — don't blend too long or it will become bitter.
exercises.question: Why shouldn't you blend too long?
options: B) It will become too hot
exercises.explanation_pt: O áudio diz 'don't blend too long or it will become bitter' = não bata por muito tempo ou ficará amargo.

exercises.transcript: For today's experiment, please follow these steps carefully. First, put on your safety goggles and gloves. Then, measure fifty milliliters of the solution using the graduated cylinder. Pour the solution into the glass beaker. Next, heat the beaker on the hot plate at medium temperature for exactly three minutes.
exercises.question: How long should you heat the beaker?
exercises.explanation_pt: As instruções dizem 'heat the beaker... for exactly three minutes' = aqueça por exatamente 3 minutos.

exercises.transcript: The library is on the north side of campus. From the main entrance, walk straight through the courtyard until you see the fountain. Turn right at the fountain and continue past the cafeteria. Take the first left after the science building. The library is the four-story building at the end of the path.
exercises.question: How many stories does the library have?
exercises.explanation_pt: O áudio diz 'four-story building' = prédio de 4 andares.

exercises.transcript: To complete your online registration, first go to the university website and click on Student Portal. Log in with your student ID and temporary password. Once you're logged in, click on Course Registration in the left menu. Search for the courses you need by department or course number. Click Add next to each course you want to take. When you've selected all your courses, click Submit Registration.
exercises.question: What should you click after selecting all courses?
exercises.explanation_pt: O áudio diz 'click Submit Registration' = clique em Enviar Inscrição.

exercises.transcript: I've been working as an emergency room nurse for about seven years now. It's one of the most challenging and rewarding jobs I can imagine. A typical shift starts at seven in the morning and lasts twelve hours. We see all kinds of cases — from minor injuries to life-threatening emergencies. The hardest part is dealing with the emotional stress, especially when we lose a patient.
exercises.question: How long is a typical shift?
exercises.explanation_pt: A enfermeira diz 'A typical shift starts at seven in the morning and lasts twelve hours' = dura 12 horas.

exercises.transcript: I grew up in a small coastal town called Paraty, about 250 kilometers from Rio de Janeiro. It's one of the most beautiful places in Brazil. The historic center has colonial architecture from the 18th century, with cobblestone streets and colorful buildings. Tourism is the main industry, especially during the literary festival in July.
exercises.question: What is the main industry in Paraty?
exercises.explanation_pt: O falante diz 'Tourism is the main industry' = turismo é a principal indústria.

exercises.transcript: Last year, I spent six months studying in Toronto, Canada. At first, it was really difficult. I could barely understand what people were saying because they spoke so fast. But after about two months, something clicked. I started thinking in English instead of translating from Portuguese in my head.
exercises.question: How long did it take for the speaker to improve significantly?
exercises.explanation_pt: O falante diz 'after about two months, something clicked' = depois de cerca de 2 meses, algo mudou.

exercises.transcript: Deforestation in the Amazon has been one of Brazil's most serious environmental problems for decades. Between August 2019 and July 2020, nearly eleven thousand square kilometers of rainforest were destroyed. The main causes are cattle ranching, which accounts for about 80 percent of deforestation, followed by soy farming, logging, and mining.
exercises.question: What is the main cause of deforestation in the Amazon?
exercises.explanation_pt: O áudio diz 'cattle ranching, which accounts for about 80 percent' = pecuária, responsável por cerca de 80%.

exercises.transcript: Artificial intelligence has changed the way we interact with technology. Virtual assistants like Siri and Alexa can answer our questions, play music, and control our home devices using just voice commands. In healthcare, AI systems can analyze medical images and detect diseases like cancer earlier than human doctors in some cases.
exercises.question: What can AI do in healthcare according to the speaker?
options: B) Detect diseases earlier than doctors in some cases
exercises.explanation_pt: O falante diz que IA pode 'detect diseases like cancer earlier than human doctors in some cases' = detectar doenças mais cedo que médicos em alguns casos.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a lecture sobre biologia e responda:
exercises.transcript: Today I want to talk about photosynthesis, which is one of the most fundamental processes in biology. Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and certain bacteria convert light energy, usually from the sun, into chemical energy in the form of glucose. This process takes place primarily in the chloroplasts. The basic equation is: carbon dioxide plus water, in the presence of light energy, produces glucose and oxygen.
exercises.question: What is the main product of photosynthesis?
exercises.explanation_pt: A lecture diz que fotossíntese 'produces glucose and oxygen' = produz glicose e oxigênio.

exercises.transcript: Let's discuss a concept that's central to developmental psychology: attachment theory. This theory was originally developed by British psychologist John Bowlby in the 1950s and later expanded by Mary Ainsworth. The core idea is that the bond between a child and their primary caregiver has a profound impact on the child's emotional and social development. Ainsworth identified three main attachment styles through her famous Strange Situation experiment.
exercises.question: Who originally developed attachment theory?
exercises.explanation_pt: A lecture diz 'originally developed by British psychologist John Bowlby in the 1950s'.

exercises.transcript: Today we're going to examine the Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century and transformed virtually every aspect of human society. Before the Industrial Revolution, most people lived in rural areas and made their living through agriculture. Then, beginning around 1760, a series of technological innovations changed everything. The steam engine, developed by James Watt, provided a new source of power that could drive machinery in factories.
exercises.question: When did the Industrial Revolution begin?
exercises.explanation_pt: A lecture diz 'beginning around 1760' = começando por volta de 1760.

exercises.transcript: I'd like to talk about black holes, one of the most fascinating objects in the universe. A black hole is a region in space where gravity is so strong that nothing — not even light — can escape from it. Black holes are formed when massive stars, typically at least twenty to twenty-five times the mass of our Sun, reach the end of their life cycle. The boundary around a black hole is called the event horizon.
exercises.question: What is the event horizon?
options: A) The center of a black hole
C) The boundary around a black hole
D) A galaxy near a black hole
exercises.explanation_pt: A lecture diz 'The boundary around a black hole is called the event horizon' = o limite ao redor de um buraco negro.

exercises.transcript: Today's topic is supply and demand, which is perhaps the most fundamental concept in economics. The law of demand states that, all else being equal, as the price of a product increases, the quantity demanded decreases. Conversely, as the price decreases, the quantity demanded increases. When these two forces interact in a market, they reach what economists call an equilibrium price.
exercises.question: According to the law of demand, what happens when the price increases?
exercises.explanation_pt: A lei da demanda diz que 'as the price increases, the quantity demanded decreases' = quando o preço sobe, a demanda cai.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a conversa entre um aluno e um professor:
exercises.transcript: Student: Professor, I'm struggling with the research paper. I've read all the required sources, but I'm having trouble organizing my arguments. Professor: That's actually a common problem. Have you tried creating an outline first? Start with your thesis statement, then list your three main points, and under each point, note the evidence you'll use to support it. Student: I haven't tried that. Should each point be a separate paragraph? Professor: Essentially, yes. Each body paragraph should focus on one main argument. And make sure to connect your points with transition words like 'furthermore,' 'however,' and 'in contrast.' Student: That makes sense. What about the conclusion? Professor: Your conclusion should restate your thesis in different words and summarize your key arguments. Don't introduce new information in the conclusion.
exercises.question: What does the professor suggest the student do first?
options: B) Create an outline with thesis and main points
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor sugere 'creating an outline first' com thesis statement e 3 pontos principais.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript do l11-01]
exercises.question: What should NOT be in the conclusion according to the professor?
options: A) A summary of key arguments
B) A restatement of the thesis
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor diz 'Don't introduce new information in the conclusion' = Não introduza informação nova na conclusão.

exercises.transcript: Student: Excuse me, Professor Kim. I noticed that I got a C on my midterm, but I thought I did better than that. Is there any way I can review my exam? Professor: Of course. Come to my office hours on Thursday between 2 and 4 PM. Bring your student ID and I'll show you where you lost points. Student: Is there any opportunity for extra credit to improve my grade? Professor: I don't offer extra credit, but your final exam is worth 40% of your grade, so a strong performance there can significantly improve your overall score. I also recommend attending the study sessions that the teaching assistant holds every Tuesday evening. Student: I didn't know about those. Where are they held? Professor: In room 205 of the science building, from 6 to 8 PM.
exercises.question: How much is the final exam worth?
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor diz 'your final exam is worth 40% of your grade'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: When are the study sessions?
exercises.explanation_pt: As sessões de estudo são 'every Tuesday evening' em 'room 205, from 6 to 8 PM'.

exercises.transcript: Student: Hi, I'm a pre-med student and I'd like to register for Organic Chemistry next semester, but the system says the class is full. Is there a waitlist? Advisor: Yes, there is. Currently there are 12 students on the waitlist, and the class capacity is 35. Historically, about 5 to 8 students drop the course in the first two weeks, so there's a reasonable chance you'll get in. Student: What if I don't get off the waitlist? Advisor: You could take it in the summer session. It's the same course, same credit hours, just condensed into eight weeks instead of sixteen. The downside is that it's more intense. Student: I see. And would it still count toward my pre-med requirements? Advisor: Absolutely. It's the same course, just offered in a different format.
exercises.question: How many students are on the waitlist?
exercises.explanation_pt: O advisor diz 'Currently there are 12 students on the waitlist'.

exercises.transcript: Today we'll discuss urbanization — the process by which populations shift from rural to urban areas. In 1950, only about 30% of the world's population lived in cities. Today, that number exceeds 55%, and by 2050, it's projected to reach nearly 70%. This rapid urbanization presents both opportunities and challenges. Cities offer better access to education, healthcare, and employment. However, they also face problems like housing shortages, traffic congestion, pollution, and social inequality. One concept I want you to understand is the 'urban sprawl' — the uncontrolled expansion of urban areas into surrounding rural land, which destroys natural habitats and increases dependence on cars.
exercises.question: What percentage of the world's population lived in cities in 1950?
exercises.explanation_pt: A lecture diz 'In 1950, only about 30% of the world's population lived in cities'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is 'urban sprawl'?
options: A) The renovation of old city buildings
B) The uncontrolled expansion of cities into rural areas
C) The increase in city population
exercises.explanation_pt: Urban sprawl é 'the uncontrolled expansion of urban areas into surrounding rural land'.

exercises.transcript: Let me introduce an important economic concept: opportunity cost. Every time you make a decision, you're giving up the next best alternative. For example, if you decide to spend four years in medical school, the opportunity cost includes the salary you could have earned working during those four years. This doesn't mean medical school is a bad decision — it means you should consider what you're giving up when making choices. Economists use opportunity cost to analyze everything from personal decisions to government policy. When a government decides to spend $10 billion on military defense, the opportunity cost might be the schools, hospitals, or infrastructure that could have been built with that money.
exercises.question: What is opportunity cost?
options: A) The price of a product
B) The cost of a missed opportunity
C) The value of the next best alternative you give up
D) The cost of making mistakes
exercises.explanation_pt: Custo de oportunidade é 'the next best alternative you give up' = o valor da melhor alternativa que você abre mão ao tomar uma decisão.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What example does the professor give about government spending?
options: B) Military spending vs schools, hospitals, and infrastructure
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor usa o exemplo de $10 bilhões em defesa militar vs escolas, hospitais e infraestrutura que poderiam ter sido construídos.

exercises.transcript: I'd like to talk about the concept of social mobility — the ability of individuals or families to move up or down the social and economic ladder. Countries with high social mobility, like Denmark and Norway, have strong public education systems, universal healthcare, and robust social safety nets. In these countries, a child born into a low-income family has a reasonable chance of achieving middle-class or upper-class status. In contrast, countries with low social mobility, like Brazil and the United States, tend to have greater income inequality and fewer public services. In these societies, your parents' income level is a strong predictor of your own future earnings — in other words, if you're born poor, you're more likely to stay poor.
exercises.question: Which countries have HIGH social mobility according to the lecture?
options: A) Brazil and the United States
exercises.explanation_pt: A lecture cita Dinamarca e Noruega como países com alta mobilidade social.

exercises.transcript: Today's lecture is about keystone species — organisms that have a disproportionately large impact on their ecosystem relative to their population size. The classic example is the sea otter along the Pacific coast of North America. Sea otters prey on sea urchins, which feed on kelp — underwater seaweed forests. When sea otters were nearly hunted to extinction in the 19th century, sea urchin populations exploded and devoured the kelp forests. Without kelp forests, hundreds of species that depended on them for food and shelter declined dramatically. When sea otters were reintroduced, sea urchin populations decreased, kelp forests recovered, and the entire ecosystem was restored. This is a perfect example of a trophic cascade — a chain of ecological effects triggered by changes at the top of the food chain.
exercises.question: What happened when sea otters were nearly hunted to extinction?
options: C) Sea urchins ate the kelp forests
exercises.explanation_pt: Sem as lontras, a população de ouriços-do-mar explodiu e devorou as florestas de kelp.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is a keystone species?
options: A) The largest species in an ecosystem
B) A species with a disproportionately large impact relative to its population
D) A species at the bottom of the food chain
exercises.explanation_pt: Keystone species = 'organisms that have a disproportionately large impact on their ecosystem relative to their population size'.

exercises.transcript: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's remarkable ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. This concept has revolutionized our understanding of the brain. Previously, scientists believed that the adult brain was essentially fixed — that brain cells could not regenerate and neural pathways, once established, could not change. We now know this is wrong. The brain continues to change and adapt in response to new experiences, learning, and even injury. For example, when someone loses their sight, the visual cortex — the area normally dedicated to processing visual information — can be repurposed to process auditory or tactile information. This is why blind individuals often develop enhanced hearing and sense of touch. The implications for rehabilitation are profound: stroke patients can recover lost functions because healthy parts of the brain can take over the roles of damaged areas.
exercises.question: What did scientists previously believe about the adult brain?
options: A) It could grow new brain cells
B) It was essentially fixed and could not change
C) It became more flexible with age
D) It was more adaptable than children's brains
exercises.explanation_pt: Anteriormente, cientistas acreditavam que o cérebro adulto 'was essentially fixed' = era essencialmente fixo.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What happens to the visual cortex of blind individuals?
options: B) It processes only visual memories
C) It is repurposed to process auditory or tactile information
exercises.explanation_pt: O córtex visual 'can be repurposed to process auditory or tactile information' = pode ser reaproveitado para processar informações auditivas ou táteis.

exercises.transcript: Let's discuss the water cycle, which is one of the most important natural processes on Earth. The water cycle, also called the hydrological cycle, describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the surface of the Earth. It begins with evaporation — when the sun heats water in oceans, lakes, and rivers, causing it to transform into water vapor that rises into the atmosphere. As this vapor rises and cools, it undergoes condensation, forming tiny water droplets that group together to form clouds. When these droplets become heavy enough, they fall back to Earth as precipitation — rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Some of this water flows over the surface as runoff into rivers and streams, eventually returning to the ocean. Some seeps into the ground, becoming groundwater that feeds wells and springs. And the cycle begins again.
exercises.question: What is the correct order of the water cycle?
exercises.explanation_pt: A ordem correta: Evaporation (evaporação) → Condensation (condensação) → Precipitation (precipitação).

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça o debate entre dois estudantes:
exercises.transcript: Student A: I think standardized tests like the TOEFL are the fairest way to evaluate language proficiency. Everyone takes the same test under the same conditions, so the results are comparable. Student B: I disagree. Standardized tests only measure how well someone performs under test conditions. They don't measure real-world communication skills. I know people who score 110 on the TOEFL but can barely hold a conversation. Student A: That's a fair point, but what's the alternative? If every university created its own evaluation, the process would be chaotic and inconsistent. Student B: I think a portfolio-based approach would be better — essays, recorded presentations, interviews. It takes more time, but it gives a more complete picture of a student's abilities. Student A: That sounds ideal in theory, but it would be incredibly expensive and difficult to standardize across institutions.
exercises.question: What is Student B's main criticism of standardized tests?
options: C) They don't measure real-world communication skills
exercises.explanation_pt: Student B diz que testes padronizados 'don't measure real-world communication skills' = não medem habilidades de comunicação no mundo real.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What alternative does Student B propose?
options: B) A portfolio with essays, presentations, and interviews
exercises.explanation_pt: Student B propõe 'a portfolio-based approach — essays, recorded presentations, interviews'.

exercises.transcript: Professor: Today we'll discuss whether zoos are ethical. Let's hear both sides. Student A: I believe zoos serve an important purpose. Many species would be extinct without zoo breeding programs. Zoos also educate the public about wildlife conservation and inspire people to care about endangered species. Student B: While that may be true for some zoos, the reality is that most animals in zoos suffer from poor mental health due to confinement. Elephants, for example, naturally roam up to 50 kilometers per day, but in zoos, they're confined to enclosures a fraction of that size. Student A: Modern zoos have improved significantly. Many now have large, naturalistic habitats and enrichment programs to keep animals stimulated. Student B: That's an improvement, but it still doesn't replicate the wild. I think resources spent on zoos would be better invested in protecting natural habitats and fighting poaching. Professor: Good arguments from both sides. This tension between conservation and animal welfare is at the heart of the zoo debate.
exercises.question: What does the professor say about the debate?
options: C) The tension between conservation and animal welfare is the core issue
D) Students should visit more zoos
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor diz que 'This tension between conservation and animal welfare is at the heart of the zoo debate'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What does Student B say about elephants?
options: A) They are happy in zoos
B) They naturally roam up to 50 km per day but are confined in zoos
C) They reproduce well in captivity
exercises.explanation_pt: Student B diz que elefantes 'naturally roam up to 50 kilometers per day, but in zoos, they're confined to enclosures a fraction of that size'.

exercises.transcript: Student A: I think social media companies should be legally responsible for the content on their platforms. If a newspaper publishes false information, it can be sued. Why should Facebook or Twitter be different? Student B: Because they're platforms, not publishers. They don't create content — users do. If we make platforms liable for every post, they'll either censor everything or shut down, which would harm free speech. Student A: But their algorithms actively promote harmful content because it generates more engagement. They're not just neutral platforms — they're amplifying the worst content. Student B: I agree that algorithms are a problem, but the solution should be algorithmic transparency, not blanket liability. Companies should be required to explain how their algorithms work, and users should have more control over what they see.
exercises.question: What does Student A want?
options: A) To shut down all social media
B) Social media companies to be legally responsible for content
exercises.explanation_pt: Student A quer que empresas de mídia social 'should be legally responsible for the content on their platforms'.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a lecture e responda (questão 1 de 2):
exercises.transcript: One of the most fascinating phenomena in animal behavior is migration. Every year, billions of animals — from monarch butterflies to Arctic terns to wildebeest — undertake long-distance journeys, often covering thousands of kilometers. But how do they navigate? Research has identified several mechanisms. Birds, for example, use a combination of the sun's position during the day, star patterns at night, and the Earth's magnetic field. Some species even have magnetite — tiny magnetic crystals — in their beaks or brains that act as a built-in compass. Sea turtles use the angle and intensity of the Earth's magnetic field to navigate across entire oceans, returning to the exact beach where they were born to lay their eggs. Monarch butterflies migrate up to 4,500 kilometers from Canada to central Mexico, despite having never made the journey before — the generation that returns north is different from the one that flew south. This suggests that migration routes are somehow encoded in their genes.
exercises.question: How do birds navigate during migration?
options: B) Using the sun, stars, and Earth's magnetic field
D) Using their sense of smell
exercises.explanation_pt: Aves usam 'a combination of the sun's position, star patterns, and the Earth's magnetic field' para navegar.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is remarkable about monarch butterfly migration?
options: A) They fly the fastest of all insects
B) They migrate in groups of millions
C) The generation that returns is different from the one that left
D) They only migrate when it's cold
exercises.explanation_pt: O notável é que 'the generation that returns north is different from the one that flew south' = a geração que retorna é diferente da que partiu. As rotas parecem ser codificadas nos genes.

exercises.transcript: Let's examine the placebo effect from a neuroscience perspective. When patients believe they are receiving effective treatment, their brains actually release endorphins and dopamine — real chemicals that reduce pain and improve mood. In clinical trials, placebos have been shown to be effective in treating conditions ranging from chronic pain to depression to Parkinson's disease. Remarkably, the placebo effect works even when patients know they are receiving a placebo — these are called 'open-label placebos.' A 2016 study at Harvard found that patients with irritable bowel syndrome who were told they were taking sugar pills still showed significant improvement compared to a control group that received no treatment. This challenges our fundamental understanding of the mind-body connection and raises important questions about the role of expectation and belief in healing.
exercises.question: What happens in the brain during the placebo effect?
options: B) The brain releases endorphins and dopamine
D) The brain stops processing pain
exercises.explanation_pt: O cérebro 'actually releases endorphins and dopamine' = realmente libera endorfinas e dopamina.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What did the 2016 Harvard study find about open-label placebos?
options: A) They don't work at all
B) They only work for pain
C) Patients improved even knowing they were taking sugar pills
exercises.explanation_pt: O estudo descobriu que pacientes que 'were told they were taking sugar pills still showed significant improvement' = sabiam que tomavam pílulas de açúcar e ainda assim melhoraram.

exercises.transcript: Today I want to discuss the concept of herd immunity, which has become particularly relevant during the COVID-19 pandemic. Herd immunity occurs when a sufficiently large portion of a population becomes immune to a disease, either through vaccination or prior infection, making the spread of the disease unlikely. The threshold for herd immunity varies by disease — for measles, which is extremely contagious, about 95% of the population needs to be immune. For COVID-19, estimates ranged from 60% to 90%. When herd immunity is achieved, even individuals who cannot be vaccinated — such as newborns, pregnant women, or people with compromised immune systems — are indirectly protected because the disease has fewer opportunities to spread. This is why vaccination is often described as both a personal and a social responsibility.
exercises.question: What percentage needs to be immune for herd immunity against measles?
exercises.explanation_pt: Para sarampo (measles), 'about 95% of the population needs to be immune' para atingir imunidade de rebanho.

exercises.transcript: Today we're going to examine the concept of 'art for art's sake' versus 'art for society's sake.' The phrase 'art for art's sake,' or 'l'art pour l'art' in French, emerged in the 19th century and argues that art's value lies purely in its aesthetic beauty — it doesn't need to serve any social, political, or moral purpose. Proponents like Oscar Wilde and Théophile Gautier believed that asking art to be useful was like asking a flower to be edible. On the opposite end of the spectrum, thinkers like Leo Tolstoy argued that art has a moral obligation to society. Tolstoy believed that true art should communicate emotions that bring people together and promote social harmony. He dismissed much of the art of his time as elitist entertainment that served no meaningful purpose. This debate remains very much alive today — think about the controversy whenever public funding goes to contemporary art installations that many people don't understand or appreciate.
exercises.question: What does 'art for art's sake' mean?
options: A) Art should always have a political message
B) Art's value lies purely in its aesthetic beauty, not social purpose
C) Art should be affordable for everyone
D) Art is only for educated people
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Art for art's sake' argumenta que o valor da arte está 'purely in its aesthetic beauty' — não precisa servir propósito social, político ou moral.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What did Tolstoy believe about art?
options: A) Art should only be beautiful
B) Art has a moral obligation to society
exercises.explanation_pt: Tolstoy acreditava que 'true art should communicate emotions that bring people together and promote social harmony' = arte tem obrigação moral com a sociedade.

exercises.transcript: Let me introduce you to the trolley problem, one of the most famous thought experiments in ethics. Imagine a trolley is heading toward five people tied to the tracks. You are standing next to a lever that can divert the trolley to a different track, where only one person is tied. Do you pull the lever? Most people say yes — saving five lives at the cost of one seems like the right thing to do. This is called utilitarian reasoning — maximizing the greatest good for the greatest number. But now consider a variation. You're standing on a bridge above the tracks. The only way to stop the trolley is to push a large man off the bridge onto the tracks, killing him but saving the five. Most people say no to this version, even though the math is the same — one death to save five. Why the difference? Philosophers call this the distinction between action and inaction, or between direct and indirect harm.
exercises.question: Why do most people refuse to push the man off the bridge?
options: A) Because they don't want to save five people
B) Because pushing someone feels like direct harm, unlike pulling a lever
C) Because the man is too heavy
D) Because the trolley would stop anyway
exercises.explanation_pt: A diferença é entre ação direta (empurrar alguém) e indireta (puxar uma alavanca). Empurrar alguém parece dano direto, mesmo que o resultado matemático seja o mesmo.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is utilitarian reasoning?
options: A) Always following rules regardless of consequences
B) Maximizing the greatest good for the greatest number
C) Doing whatever feels right emotionally
exercises.explanation_pt: Raciocínio utilitarista = 'maximizing the greatest good for the greatest number' = maximizar o maior bem para o maior número de pessoas.

exercises.transcript: The Renaissance artist Leonardo da Vinci is often described as the ultimate 'Renaissance man' — someone with deep expertise in multiple fields. While he is most famous for paintings like the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper, da Vinci's notebooks reveal that he was equally accomplished as a scientist, engineer, and inventor. His anatomical drawings, based on actual dissections of human bodies, were so accurate that they are still used in medical textbooks today. He designed flying machines, armored vehicles, and even a rudimentary calculator — centuries before these inventions became reality. What made da Vinci exceptional was not just his talent in individual fields, but his ability to see connections between them. He believed that art and science were inseparable — that understanding anatomy made him a better painter, and that artistic observation made him a better scientist.
exercises.question: What made Leonardo da Vinci exceptional according to the lecture?
options: A) He was the richest person of his time
B) His ability to see connections between art and science
C) He painted faster than other artists
D) He traveled to many countries
exercises.explanation_pt: O excepcional de Da Vinci era 'his ability to see connections between them' = sua capacidade de ver conexões entre arte e ciência.

exercises.transcript: Today we'll discuss biomimicry — the practice of solving engineering problems by imitating designs found in nature. One of the most famous examples is the bullet train in Japan. Engineers struggled with a serious problem: when the train exited tunnels at high speed, it created a loud sonic boom that disturbed nearby residents. The solution came from an unexpected source — the kingfisher bird. Kingfishers dive from air into water at high speed without creating a splash because of the unique shape of their beaks. Engineer Eiji Nakatsu, who was also a birdwatcher, redesigned the front of the train to mimic the kingfisher's beak shape. The result was remarkable: the train became 10% faster, used 15% less electricity, and the sonic boom problem was completely eliminated.
exercises.question: What problem did the Japanese bullet train have?
options: B) It created sonic booms when exiting tunnels
C) It used too much fuel
D) It was too noisy inside
exercises.explanation_pt: O problema era que o trem 'created a loud sonic boom that disturbed nearby residents' quando saía de túneis.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What inspired the solution?
exercises.explanation_pt: A solução foi inspirada no bico do pássaro martin-pescador (kingfisher), que entra na água sem criar splash.

exercises.transcript: Let's talk about 3D printing, also known as additive manufacturing. Unlike traditional manufacturing, which typically involves cutting or molding material into shape — a subtractive process — 3D printing builds objects layer by layer from the bottom up. The technology works by following a digital blueprint, depositing material one thin layer at a time until the complete object is formed. Materials range from plastic and metal to concrete and even living cells. In medicine, 3D printing is being used to create custom prosthetics, dental implants, and surgical models that allow doctors to practice complex operations before performing them on patients. Perhaps most excitingly, researchers are working on bioprinting — using living cells as the 'ink' to print functional human organs. While we're still years away from printing a functioning heart or liver, scientists have already successfully printed skin, cartilage, and small blood vessels.
exercises.question: What is the key difference between 3D printing and traditional manufacturing?
options: B) 3D printing builds layer by layer (additive) vs cutting/molding (subtractive)
D) Traditional manufacturing is more expensive
exercises.explanation_pt: 3D printing é aditivo (builds layer by layer) enquanto manufatura tradicional é subtrativa (cutting or molding).

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What has already been successfully bioprinted?
options: C) Skin, cartilage, and small blood vessels
exercises.explanation_pt: Cientistas já imprimiram 'skin, cartilage, and small blood vessels' = pele, cartilagem e pequenos vasos sanguíneos.

exercises.transcript: I want to discuss the concept of the smart grid — a modernized electricity network that uses digital technology to monitor and manage the flow of electricity from all generation sources to meet the varying demands of end users. Traditional power grids are one-directional: electricity flows from power plants to consumers. Smart grids, however, are bidirectional. This means that a homeowner with solar panels can not only consume electricity from the grid but also sell excess energy back to the utility company. Smart grids use sensors, meters, and digital communications to detect and react to local changes in usage, reducing waste and improving reliability. They can automatically reroute power around damaged areas, reducing blackout times from hours to seconds.
exercises.question: What makes smart grids different from traditional grids?
options: B) They are bidirectional — electricity flows both ways
D) They only work with solar energy
exercises.explanation_pt: Smart grids são bidirecionais — 'electricity flows both ways', permitindo que consumidores com painéis solares vendam energia de volta.

theme: Lectures TOEFL — múltiplas perguntas por lecture

exercises.transcript: Today I want to discuss the concept of 'nudge theory' in behavioral economics. A nudge is a subtle change in how choices are presented that predictably alters people's behavior without restricting their options or significantly changing their economic incentives. The term was popularized by Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein in their 2008 book 'Nudge.' A classic example is organ donation. In countries where you must opt in to be a donor — like the United States — donation rates are typically around 15-20%. In countries where you are automatically enrolled as a donor but can opt out — like Austria and Spain — rates exceed 90%. The default option dramatically affects behavior, even though people are free to choose either way. Another example is the placement of food in cafeterias. When healthier options are placed at eye level and at the beginning of the serving line, people choose them more often — without being told what to eat. Governments around the world have established 'nudge units' to apply these insights to public policy, from encouraging tax compliance to promoting energy conservation.
exercises.question: What is a 'nudge' according to the lecture?
options: A) A law that forces people to behave differently
B) A subtle change in how choices are presented that alters behavior
C) A financial incentive to change behavior
D) A punishment for bad behavior
exercises.explanation_pt: Um nudge é 'a subtle change in how choices are presented that predictably alters people's behavior without restricting their options'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is the organ donation rate in opt-out countries like Austria?
exercises.explanation_pt: Em países opt-out como Áustria e Espanha, as taxas de doação 'exceed 90%' = excedem 90%.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is the cafeteria example designed to illustrate?
options: A) That people always choose unhealthy food
B) That placing healthy food at eye level increases its selection
C) That cafeterias should remove unhealthy food
D) That food placement doesn't matter
exercises.explanation_pt: Quando opções saudáveis ficam na altura dos olhos e no início da fila, 'people choose them more often' — sem proibir nada.

exercises.transcript: Linguistic anthropologists have long been interested in how language shapes social identity. One fascinating area of study is 'code-switching' — the practice of alternating between two or more languages or language varieties within a single conversation. In the United States, many African Americans engage in code-switching between African American Vernacular English, known as AAVE, and Standard American English, depending on the social context. A person might use AAVE with friends and family but switch to Standard English in a job interview or academic setting. This is not a sign of linguistic deficiency — in fact, research shows that effective code-switching requires high proficiency in both varieties and sophisticated social awareness. The speaker must constantly assess the social situation and choose the appropriate linguistic variety. Some scholars argue that code-switching is a form of linguistic capital — a valuable skill that allows speakers to navigate different social worlds and build connections across cultural boundaries.
exercises.question: What does code-switching require according to researchers?
options: B) High proficiency in both languages and social awareness
exercises.explanation_pt: Code-switching 'requires high proficiency in both varieties and sophisticated social awareness'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: Why do some scholars call code-switching 'linguistic capital'?
options: A) Because it makes people money
B) Because it is a valuable skill for navigating different social worlds
C) Because it is taught in universities
exercises.explanation_pt: É chamado de 'linguistic capital' porque é 'a valuable skill that allows speakers to navigate different social worlds and build connections across cultural boundaries'.

exercises.transcript: Student: Professor, I noticed that the reading for next week is about the decline of the Roman Empire. I actually wrote a paper on this topic last semester for my history class. Would it be okay if I used some of that research for our assignment? Professor: Well, I appreciate your honesty in asking. Using your own previous work without disclosure is technically considered self-plagiarism in academic contexts. However, you're certainly welcome to build upon your earlier research — just make sure you cite your previous paper properly and that the new assignment adds substantial new analysis. Student: I understand. So I can use the same sources but need to write new analysis? Professor: Exactly. Think of your previous paper as one of your sources, not as a template.
exercises.question: What can be inferred about the professor's attitude toward the student?
options: A) The professor is angry at the student
B) The professor appreciates the student's honesty and gives clear guidance
C) The professor doesn't care about plagiarism
D) The professor wants the student to start from scratch
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor diz 'I appreciate your honesty in asking' e dá orientação clara sobre como usar o trabalho anterior. Não proíbe, mas estabelece regras.

exercises.transcript: Professor: So let me ask the class — why do you think the domestication of wheat was so important for the development of civilization? Student A: Because it provided a reliable food source? Professor: That's part of it, but think bigger. What does a reliable food source allow? Student B: People could settle in one place instead of being nomadic? Professor: Getting warmer. And what happens when people settle down? Student A: They build cities? Professor: Yes! And what does that lead to? Student B: Specialization of labor — not everyone needs to farm, so some people can become craftspeople, priests, soldiers, scholars... Professor: Exactly! And that's why some historians argue that civilization as we know it began with a single grain of wheat.
exercises.question: What does the professor imply by saying 'think bigger'?
options: A) The answer should be about bigger farms
B) The student's answer is partially correct but misses the broader implications
D) The professor wants a longer answer
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Think bigger' implica que a resposta do aluno está parcialmente correta mas não captura as implicações maiores — como o surgimento de cidades e civilização.

exercises.transcript: Professor: I've noticed that about half the class didn't submit the homework on time this week. I want to remind everyone that late submissions receive a 10% deduction per day. Now, I understand that this is a challenging semester, and several of you have mentioned personal circumstances. If you're dealing with something that's affecting your academic work, I encourage you to speak with me during office hours. I can't extend deadlines retroactively, but we can discuss options going forward. I'd rather we find a solution together than have your grade suffer in silence.
exercises.question: What is the professor's tone?
options: C) Firm but supportive — enforcing rules while offering help
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor mantém as regras ('10% deduction per day') mas também oferece suporte ('speak with me during office hours', 'find a solution together'). Tom firme mas empático.

exercises.transcript: Student: So you're saying that even though the study had a large sample size and was published in a reputable journal, we shouldn't necessarily trust its conclusions? Professor: What I'm saying is that you should always read research critically. This particular study was funded by the sugar industry, and the researchers had financial ties to the companies whose products they were evaluating. Does that automatically invalidate the findings? Not necessarily. But it should make you ask harder questions about the methodology and interpretation of results.
exercises.question: What does the professor imply about industry-funded research?
options: C) It should be examined more critically due to potential bias
D) It is better than independent research
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor não diz que é automaticamente inválido, mas que 'it should make you ask harder questions' = deve ser examinado com mais senso crítico por possível viés.

exercises.transcript: Professor: Before we wrap up, I want to mention something about the final project. Several groups have come to me with very ambitious proposals — one group wants to analyze the entire healthcare system of three countries, another wants to survey 500 students about their study habits. I appreciate the enthusiasm, but remember: this is a semester project, not a doctoral dissertation. I'd rather see a focused, well-executed analysis of a narrow topic than a superficial overview of a broad one. Quality over quantity, always.
exercises.question: What is the professor's main message?
options: A) Students should make their projects bigger
B) Students should narrow their project scope and focus on quality
C) The final project is not important
D) Students should work alone, not in groups
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor quer projetos mais focados: 'a focused, well-executed analysis of a narrow topic' em vez de 'a superficial overview of a broad one'. Qualidade sobre quantidade.

theme: Simulado TOEFL Listening — lecture completa com 5 perguntas

exercises.instruction_pt: SIMULADO — Ouça a lecture completa (Q1 de 5):
exercises.transcript: Today I'd like to discuss the concept of 'tragedy of the commons,' first articulated by ecologist Garrett Hardin in 1968. Imagine a shared pasture — a commons — where multiple herders graze their cattle. Each herder benefits individually by adding one more cow to the pasture. But if every herder does this, the pasture becomes overgrazed and eventually destroyed, harming everyone. The tragedy is that rational individual behavior leads to collective disaster. Hardin used this model to explain environmental problems like overfishing, air pollution, and deforestation. When a resource is shared and unregulated, individuals have an incentive to exploit it before others do, even though this leads to its depletion. The oceans are a perfect modern example. International waters belong to no single nation, so fishing fleets from many countries compete to catch as much as possible. The result? According to the FAO, over one-third of global fish stocks are now overfished. So how do we solve the tragedy of the commons? Hardin originally suggested two solutions: privatization, where shared resources are divided into private property, or government regulation. But Nobel Prize-winning economist Elinor Ostrom demonstrated a third possibility: communities can successfully manage shared resources through self-governance — establishing their own rules, monitoring systems, and penalties for violations — without needing either privatization or top-down government control. Ostrom studied communities around the world — from fishing villages in Turkey to irrigation systems in Nepal — and found that many had sustained shared resources for centuries through cooperative management.
exercises.question: What is the 'tragedy of the commons'?
options: A) When a government takes private property
B) When rational individual behavior leads to collective destruction of shared resources
C) When people refuse to share resources
D) When resources are too abundant
exercises.explanation_pt: A tragédia dos comuns é quando 'rational individual behavior leads to collective disaster' na exploração de recursos compartilhados.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What modern example does the professor give?
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor usa oceanos/pesca como exemplo moderno: 'over one-third of global fish stocks are now overfished'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What were Hardin's two original solutions?
exercises.explanation_pt: Hardin sugeriu 'privatization' (privatização) e 'government regulation' (regulação governamental).

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What was Elinor Ostrom's contribution?
options: A) She proved the tragedy of the commons doesn't exist
B) She showed communities can self-govern shared resources without privatization or government control
C) She invented a new type of fishing net
D) She argued for complete privatization
exercises.explanation_pt: Ostrom demonstrou que comunidades podem 'successfully manage shared resources through self-governance' — sem privatização nem controle governamental.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What can be inferred about the professor's view on solving environmental problems?
options: A) Only government can solve them
C) Multiple approaches exist, including community self-governance
D) Environmental problems cannot be solved
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor apresenta três soluções (privatização, regulação, autogovernança comunitária), sugerindo que múltiplas abordagens existem. A ênfase em Ostrom sugere que autogovernança é uma opção viável e frequentemente negligenciada.

theme: TOEFL Listening — Lectures com detalhes e inferências

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a lecture sobre ecologia marinha (Q1 de 3):
exercises.transcript: I'd like to discuss the concept of 'ocean dead zones' — areas where oxygen levels are so low that most marine life cannot survive. The largest dead zone in the world is in the Gulf of Mexico, covering approximately 16,000 square kilometers every summer. Dead zones are primarily caused by nutrient pollution — when excess nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural fertilizers wash into rivers and eventually reach the ocean. These nutrients fuel massive algal blooms. When the algae die and decompose, the process consumes enormous amounts of dissolved oxygen, creating hypoxic conditions. Fish and mobile organisms flee the area, but bottom-dwelling creatures like crabs, worms, and shellfish cannot escape and often die in massive numbers. The number of dead zones worldwide has quadrupled since the 1950s, from approximately 50 to over 400 today. Climate change is expected to worsen the problem, as warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen.
exercises.question: What causes ocean dead zones?
options: B) Nutrient pollution from agricultural fertilizers causing algal blooms
exercises.explanation_pt: Dead zones são causadas por 'nutrient pollution — excess nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural fertilizers' que alimentam explosões de algas.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: How has the number of dead zones changed since the 1950s?
exercises.explanation_pt: O número 'has quadrupled since the 1950s, from approximately 50 to over 400' = quadruplicou.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: Why will climate change worsen dead zones?
options: A) It will increase fish populations
B) Warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen
C) It will reduce algae growth
D) It will decrease fertilizer use
exercises.explanation_pt: 'Warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen' = água mais quente retém menos oxigênio dissolvido, piorando as condições hipóxicas.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a lecture sobre psicologia (Q1 de 2):
exercises.transcript: Let me tell you about a fascinating study known as the marshmallow test, conducted by Walter Mischel at Stanford University in the late 1960s. In this experiment, young children were placed in a room with a single marshmallow on a table. They were told they could eat the marshmallow immediately, or if they waited 15 minutes without eating it, they would receive two marshmallows. The researchers then tracked these children for decades. Those who were able to delay gratification — to resist the temptation and wait for the larger reward — went on to have higher SAT scores, lower rates of obesity, better social skills, and greater career success. However, more recent research has complicated this picture. A 2018 study by Tyler Watts found that when researchers controlled for socioeconomic background, the correlation between delayed gratification and later success was much weaker. Children from wealthy families had more experience with reliable promises — their parents usually followed through — so waiting felt less risky. Children from less stable backgrounds had learned that waiting often meant losing out entirely.
exercises.question: What did the original marshmallow test find?
options: A) All children ate the marshmallow immediately
B) Children who waited had better life outcomes decades later
C) Marshmallows are unhealthy for children
exercises.explanation_pt: O teste original encontrou que crianças que conseguiram esperar 'went on to have higher SAT scores, lower rates of obesity, better social skills, and greater career success'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: How did the 2018 study change our understanding?
options: A) It confirmed the original results completely
B) It showed socioeconomic background, not just willpower, explains the results
C) It proved the test was fake
D) It showed that all children can wait equally
exercises.explanation_pt: O estudo de 2018 mostrou que 'when researchers controlled for socioeconomic background, the correlation was much weaker' — o contexto socioeconômico, não apenas força de vontade, explica os resultados.

theme: TOEFL Listening — Conversas no campus com nuance

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a conversa no campus (Q1 de 3):
exercises.transcript: Student: Hi Professor Clarke, do you have a minute? I wanted to talk about my thesis topic. Professor: Of course, come in. What are you thinking? Student: Well, I'm interested in researching the impact of social media on political polarization. But I'm not sure if the topic is too broad. Professor: That's a valid concern. Political polarization is an enormous field. You'd need to narrow it considerably. What specific aspect interests you most? Student: I'm particularly interested in how algorithm-driven content feeds create echo chambers — where people only see information that confirms their existing beliefs. Professor: Now that's much more focused. I'd suggest narrowing it even further to one platform and one country. For example, you could examine how Facebook's news feed algorithm contributed to political polarization in Brazil during the 2022 elections. Student: That's a great idea. It's specific enough to be manageable, and I could access data through Facebook's Ad Library. Professor: Exactly. And there's strong academic precedent — several recent papers have examined similar questions in the US and European contexts. You'd be filling a gap in the literature by focusing on Brazil. Student: Should I use qualitative or quantitative methods? Professor: I'd recommend a mixed-methods approach — content analysis of the most shared political posts combined with surveys of Facebook users about their news consumption habits.
exercises.question: What was the student's original problem with the thesis topic?
options: C) The professor didn't like it
D) It had been done before
exercises.explanation_pt: O aluno estava preocupado que o tópico 'is too broad' = é amplo demais. O professor confirmou: 'You'd need to narrow it considerably'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What specific topic does the professor suggest?
options: B) Facebook's algorithm and political polarization in Brazil during the 2022 elections
C) Twitter's impact on American politics
D) Instagram's effect on mental health
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor sugere 'how Facebook's news feed algorithm contributed to political polarization in Brazil during the 2022 elections'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What research methodology does the professor recommend?
options: C) Mixed methods — content analysis + surveys
exercises.explanation_pt: O professor recomenda 'a mixed-methods approach — content analysis combined with surveys'.

exercises.transcript: Student: Excuse me, I'm having trouble accessing the online journal database from off-campus. Every time I try to log in, it says my credentials have expired. Librarian: That happens sometimes at the beginning of each semester. Your library access is tied to your course enrollment, and there's often a delay between registering for classes and the system updating your access. When did you register? Student: About a week ago. Librarian: That should be enough time. Let me check your account... I see the problem. You're a graduate student, but the system has you classified as an undergraduate. That limits which databases you can access. I'll submit a ticket to IT to have this corrected. In the meantime, I can give you a temporary guest login that will give you full access for 48 hours. Student: That would be great, thank you. I have a literature review due on Monday. Librarian: No problem. Also, are you aware that we offer workshops on effective database searching? Many graduate students find them helpful for conducting systematic literature reviews. Student: I didn't know that. When are they? Librarian: Every other Wednesday at 3 PM in room 106. The next one is this Wednesday.
exercises.question: What is the actual problem with the student's access?
options: B) The system classified them as undergraduate instead of graduate
exercises.explanation_pt: O problema real é que 'the system has you classified as an undergraduate' = o sistema classificou o aluno como graduação em vez de pós-graduação, limitando o acesso.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What temporary solution does the librarian offer?
options: B) A guest login with full access for 48 hours
D) Print the articles for the student
exercises.explanation_pt: A bibliotecária oferece 'a temporary guest login that will give you full access for 48 hours' enquanto o problema é corrigido pelo TI.

theme: TOEFL Listening — Lectures com múltiplos conceitos

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a lecture sobre evolução (Q1 de 3):
exercises.transcript: Today I want to discuss a concept that often confuses students: the difference between convergent and divergent evolution. Convergent evolution occurs when unrelated species independently develop similar features because they face similar environmental challenges. For example, sharks and dolphins look remarkably similar — both have streamlined bodies, dorsal fins, and flippers — but sharks are fish and dolphins are mammals. They evolved these similar features independently because both adapted to the same environment: fast movement through water. Divergent evolution is the opposite process. It occurs when closely related species develop different traits because they adapt to different environments. The classic example is Darwin's finches on the Galápagos Islands. All 13 species descended from a common ancestor, but they developed different beak shapes to exploit different food sources — some evolved large beaks for cracking seeds, others developed long beaks for probing cactus flowers, and others evolved small beaks for catching insects.
exercises.question: What is convergent evolution?
options: A) Related species developing different traits
B) Unrelated species developing similar features due to similar environments
C) Species that share a common ancestor
D) Species that live in the same area
exercises.explanation_pt: Evolução convergente é quando 'unrelated species independently develop similar features because they face similar environmental challenges'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: Why do sharks and dolphins look similar even though they are not related?
options: A) They share a common ancestor
B) They live in different environments
C) They both adapted to fast movement through water
exercises.explanation_pt: Ambos desenvolveram características similares porque 'both adapted to the same environment: fast movement through water' = convergência evolutiva.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: What is the relationship between Darwin's finches?
options: A) They are unrelated species that look similar
B) They all descended from a common ancestor but developed different traits
C) They all eat the same food
D) They migrated from different continents
exercises.explanation_pt: Os tentilhões de Darwin 'all descended from a common ancestor, but developed different beak shapes' = evolução divergente.

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a lecture sobre geologia (Q1 de 2):
exercises.transcript: The theory of plate tectonics revolutionized geology in the mid-20th century. The Earth's outer shell — the lithosphere — is divided into several large plates that float on the semi-fluid asthenosphere beneath them. These plates move very slowly, typically a few centimeters per year, driven by convection currents in the mantle. When plates collide, one may be forced beneath the other in a process called subduction, creating deep ocean trenches and volcanic mountain ranges. The Andes mountains, for example, formed where the Nazca Plate subducts beneath the South American Plate. When plates pull apart, magma rises to fill the gap, creating new oceanic crust — this is happening right now along the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the Atlantic Ocean is widening by about 2.5 centimeters per year. Interestingly, the same mechanism that creates new crust at ridges destroys old crust at subduction zones, meaning the ocean floor is continuously being recycled. The oldest ocean floor is only about 200 million years old, while continental crust can be over 4 billion years old.
exercises.question: What happens at subduction zones?
options: B) One plate is forced beneath another, creating trenches and volcanoes
exercises.explanation_pt: Em zonas de subducção, 'one may be forced beneath the other, creating deep ocean trenches and volcanic mountain ranges'.

exercises.transcript: [Mesmo transcript]
exercises.question: Why is the ocean floor younger than continental crust?
options: A) The ocean is newer than the continents
B) Ocean crust is continuously recycled — created at ridges and destroyed at subduction zones
D) The ocean floor doesn't age
exercises.explanation_pt: O fundo oceânico é continuamente reciclado: criado em dorsais oceânicas e destruído em zonas de subducção. Por isso é mais 'jovem' (máx 200 milhões de anos) que a crosta continental (4+ bilhões).

theme: TOEFL Listening — Conversas com serviços do campus

exercises.instruction_pt: Ouça a conversa com o departamento financeiro (Q1 de 3):
exercises.transcript: Student: Hi, I received a notice saying my tuition payment is overdue, but I'm on a scholarship that should cover everything. Financial Aid Officer: Let me pull up your account... I see. Your scholarship covers tuition and fees, but there's a $350 charge for the international student health insurance, which isn't included in your scholarship package. Student: Oh, I didn't realize that. Can I use my own insurance from my home country instead? Officer: Yes, you can apply for an insurance waiver, but you'll need to submit proof that your existing insurance meets the university's minimum coverage requirements. The waiver application is on the student portal under 'Financial Services.' Student: What are the minimum requirements? Officer: Your insurance must cover at least $100,000 per illness or injury, include mental health services, and provide medical evacuation coverage. You'll also need to have the insurance company send a letter in English confirming these details. Student: And if my insurance doesn't meet all those requirements?
